sections.vendors: Still no new factory list published — the version at asosplc.com remains dated 1 April 2026 (182 suppliers, 503 factories, 30 countries, 85%+ of production in China, Eastern Europe, India, Turkey and the UK). This is now the second consecutive week the quarterly refresh has been overdue, so a July edition is increasingly likely soon.
sections.strategy: ASOS pushed further into its 2026 brand relaunch, built around a 'product creator' model, AI-driven personalization and influencer-led social amplification aimed at winning back 18-34 shoppers lost to ultra-fast-fashion rivals. Management continues to describe the turnaround as 'well under way,' citing inventory down 60% versus FY22, fewer markdowns, and 100+ new partner brands added to the marketplace since FY25's 15% revenue decline (£2.9bn to £2.5bn).
sections.pricing: Heavy July promotional activity continued, with around 16 active discount codes live including offers up to 50% off — consistent with ASOS's markdown-light-but-promo-heavy stance since tightening inventory.
sections.launches: ASOS and Deliveroo's on-demand football shirt drop (ASOS DESIGN, £12-£22, 25-minute delivery across Greater London and Manchester) continued running through 19 July, covering national designs including England, Spain, Brazil and Argentina. ASOS also opened a surf-style pop-up in the Hamptons this month, extending its post-NYC physical retail push and testing demand for casual categories like denim and T-shirts.
sections.gossip: Analyst sentiment stayed constructive: JPMorgan's price target sits at £2.85 (up from £2.40) and Deutsche Bank's at £4.00 (up from £3.75), with consensus around £3.51 — continued upward revisions reflecting confidence in the turnaround despite the FY25 revenue drop.
soWhat: The factory list refresh is now overdue for a second week running — suppliers in China, Turkey, India and Bangladesh should expect a July/August 2026 PDF and watch for tier or facility changes when it lands.

The product-creator/relaunch strategy and Deliveroo-style rapid drops point to ASOS wanting smaller-batch, faster-turnaround production capability — a signal for suppliers who can support quick-response manufacturing.
Sustained promotional intensity alongside inventory discipline suggests continued cost-of-goods pressure on suppliers even as analyst confidence in the turnaround improves.
